source: CIS GOOGLE KUBERNETES ENGINE (GKE) BENCHMARK V1.7.0 PDF
title: Ensure use of Binary Authorization

input_row.source: CIS GOOGLE KUBERNETES ENGINE (GKE) BENCHMARK V1.7.0 PDF
input_row.title: Ensure use of Binary Authorization
input_row.description: Binary Authorization helps to protect supply-chain security by only allowing images with verifiable cryptographically signed metadata into the cluster.
input_row.rationale: Binary Authorization provides software supply-chain security for images that are deployed to GKE from Google Container Registry (GCR) or another container image registry. Binary Authorization requires images to be signed by trusted authorities during the development process. These signatures are then validated at deployment time. By enforcing validation, tighter control over the container environment can be gained by ensuring only verified images are integrated into the build-and-release process. Impact: Care must be taken when defining policy in order to prevent inadvertent denial of container image deployments. Depending on policy, attestations for existing container images running within the cluster may need to be created before those images are redeployed or pulled as part of the pod churn. To prevent key system images from being denied deployment, consider the use of global policy evaluation mode, which uses a global policy provided by Google and exempts a list of Google-provided system images from further policy evaluation.
input_row.audit: Using Google Cloud Console: To check that Binary Authorization is enabled for the GKE cluster: 1. Go to the Kubernetes Engine by visiting: https://console.cloud.google.com/kubernetes/list 2. Select the cluster for which Binary Authorization is disabled. 3. Under the details pane, within the Security section, ensure that 'Binary Authorization' is set to 'Enabled'. Then, assess the contents of the policy: 4. Go to Binary Authorization by visiting: https://console.cloud.google.com/security/binary-authorization 5. Ensure a policy is defined and the project default rule is not configured to 'Allow all images'. Using Command Line: To check that Binary Authorization is enabled for the GKE cluster: gcloud container clusters describe <cluster_name> --zone <compute_zone> -- format json | jq .binaryAuthorization The above command output will be the following if Binary Authorization is enabled: { "enabled": true } Then, assess the contents of the policy: gcloud container binauthz policy export > current-policy.yaml Ensure that the current policy is not configured to allow all images (evaluationMode: ALWAYS_ALLOW): cat current-policy.yaml ... defaultAdmissionRule: evaluationMode: ALWAYS_ALLOW
input_row.remediation: Using Google Cloud Console 1. Go to Binary Authorization by visiting: https://console.cloud.google.com/security/binary-authorization. 2. Enable the Binary Authorization API (if disabled). 3. Create an appropriate policy for use with the cluster. See https://cloud.google.com/binary-authorization/docs/policy-yaml-reference for guidance. 4. Go to Kubernetes Engine by visiting: https://console.cloud.google.com/kubernetes/list. 5. Select the cluster for which Binary Authorization is disabled. 6. Under the details pane, within the Security section, click on the pencil icon named Edit Binary Authorization. 7. Check the box next to Enable Binary Authorization. 8. Choose Enforce policy and provide a directory for the policy to be used. 9. Click SAVE CHANGES. Using Command Line: Update the cluster to enable Binary Authorization: gcloud container cluster update <cluster_name> --zone <compute_zone> -- binauthz-evaluation-mode=<evaluation_mode> Example: gcloud container clusters update $CLUSTER_NAME --zone $COMPUTE_ZONE -- binauthz-evaluation-mode=PROJECT_SINGLETON_POLICY_ENFORCE See: https://cloud.google.com/sdk/gcloud/reference/container/clusters/update#-- binauthz-evaluation-mode for more details around the evaluation modes available. Create a Binary Authorization Policy using the Binary Authorization Policy Reference: https://cloud.google.com/binary-authorization/docs/policy-yaml-reference for guidance. Import the policy file into Binary Authorization: gcloud container binauthz policy import <yaml_policy> Default Value: By default, Binary Authorization is disabled. References: 1. https://cloud.google.com/binary-authorization/docs/setting-up 2. https://cloud.google.com/sdk/gcloud/reference/container/clusters/update#-- binauthz-evaluation-mode
input_row.impact: Care must be taken when defining policy in order to prevent inadvertent denial of container image deployments. Depending on policy, attestations for existing container images running within the cluster may need to be created before those images are redeployed or pulled as part of the pod churn. To prevent key system images from being denied deployment, consider the use of global policy evaluation mode, which uses a global policy provided by Google and exempts a list of Google-provided system images from further policy evaluation.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
The control regarding the use of Binary Authorization for GKE can be fully verified via GCP APIs and the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud). The audit field provides explicit instructions on how to use the `gcloud` command to verify if Binary Authorization is enabled and how to export and assess the policy. This confirms that the process can be automated without the need for manual intervention.

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_gke_cluster_binary_authorization_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
The automated program will perform the following checks:

1. **Verify Binary Authorization Enabled for GKE Clusters:**
   - Use `gcloud container clusters describe <cluster_name> --zone <compute_zone> --format json` to check the `binaryAuthorization` field.
   - Validate that the field has `"enabled": true`.

2. **Inspect Binary Authorization Policy:**
   - Export the current policy using `gcloud container binauthz policy export > current-policy.yaml`.
   - Read and parse `current-policy.yaml` to verify that the `defaultAdmissionRule.evaluationMode` is not set to `ALWAYS_ALLOW`, ensuring policies enforce signature verification.

3. **Pass/Fail Criteria:**
   - **Pass:** Binary Authorization is enabled (`"enabled": true`) and the `evaluationMode` is set to a mode that requires signature checks (such as `REQUIRE_ATTESTATION` or `REQUIRE_ALL_ATTESTATIONS`).
   - **Fail:** Binary Authorization is disabled or the policy allows all images without restriction (`evaluationMode: ALWAYS_ALLOW`).

By performing these steps, the program ensures that only container images with verifiable signatures are allowed, maintaining the integrity of the supply chain within the GKE environment.

**SOURCE:** CIS GOOGLE KUBERNETES ENGINE (GKE) BENCHMARK V1.7.0 PDF
step2_review:
---

### 1. APPROACH VALIDATION: **AGREE**

- **Manual vs Automated Classification:**  
  The classification as "Automated" is correct. Both the GKE cluster configuration and the Binary Authorization policy can be programmatically checked using GCP APIs and the `gcloud` CLI, without requiring manual inspection.
- **GCP SDK Capabilities:**  
  The `gcloud` CLI supports both cluster configuration inspection and Binary Authorization policy export. The same can be achieved via REST APIs (`projects.locations.clusters.get` for GKE, and `projects.policy.get` for Binary Authorization).
- **Other GCP Tools:**  
  While CloudFormation is AWS-specific, GCP alternatives like Deployment Manager or Terraform are not directly relevant for audit checks, but GCP Config (now "Config Controller") could be used for continuous compliance, though not required for this control.

---

### 2. TECHNICAL ACCURACY: **Score: 8/10**

- **Strengths:**
  - The `gcloud container clusters describe` command is correct for fetching cluster configuration.
  - The `binaryAuthorization` field is present in the cluster spec and can be checked for enablement.
  - Exporting the Binary Authorization policy with `gcloud container binauthz policy export` is accurate.
  - The logic for checking `evaluationMode` is correct: it must not be `ALWAYS_ALLOW` to enforce signature verification.
- **Weaknesses:**
  - The field in the cluster spec is `binaryAuthorization: { enabled: true }` only for clusters using the legacy API. For Autopilot or newer clusters, the field may be structured differently (`binaryAuthorization: { ... }`). The check should be robust to field variations.
  - The check for `evaluationMode` should also consider project-level and cluster-level policies. The control is not fully met if the project-level policy is restrictive but the cluster disables enforcement.
  - The program name is descriptive but could be more explicit (see below).

---

### 3. IMPROVEMENTS

**a. Technical Improvements:**
- **Cluster Binary Authorization Field:**  
  The cluster config may have `binaryAuthorization: { enabled: true }` or `binaryAuthorization: { evaluationMode: ... }` depending on GKE version and API. The script should handle both.
- **Policy Scope:**  
  The audit should ensure that the project-level Binary Authorization policy is enforced and that clusters do not override it to weaken enforcement.
- **Multiple Clusters:**  
  The script should iterate over all clusters in all regions/zones, not just a single cluster.
- **API Alternative:**  
  For automation, consider using the REST API (`projects.locations.clusters.get` and `projects.policy.get`) for integration with SIEM or compliance tools.
- **Policy Evaluation:**  
  The check for `evaluationMode` should ensure it's set to `REQUIRE_ATTESTATION` or similar, not just "not ALWAYS_ALLOW".

**b. Program Name:**
- **Current:** `gcp_gke_cluster_binary_authorization_enabled`
- **Suggested:**  
  - `gcp_gke_cluster_binary_authorization_enforced`
  - `gcp_gke_cluster_binary_authorization_policy_enforcement`
  - `gcp_gke_cluster_image_signature_verification_enforced`
- The suggested names are more explicit about enforcement and signature verification, not just enablement.

---

### 4. CONFIDENCE: **HIGH**

- The assessment is fundamentally sound and leverages the correct GCP APIs and CLI commands. Minor improvements are needed for robustness and completeness, but the approach is valid and technically accurate.

---

### 5. FINAL RECOMMENDATION: **Modify**

- **Keep as Automated.**
- **Modify** the automation script to:
  - Handle all clusters in all regions/zones.
  - Accommodate variations in the `binaryAuthorization` field.
  - Ensure both cluster-level and project-level policies are enforced.
  - Update the program name to reflect enforcement, not just enablement.
- **Documentation:**  
  Add a note about differences in field structure for various GKE versions and consider using the REST API for broader automation.

---

**Summary Table**

| Section            | Decision/Score | Notes                                                                                   |
|--------------------|---------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Approach Validation| AGREE          | Fully automatable via gcloud/API                                                        |
| Technical Accuracy | 8/10           | Correct commands, minor field handling and completeness issues                          |
| Improvements       | -              | Handle all clusters, field variations, policy scope, better program name                |
| Confidence         | HIGH           | Approach is fundamentally correct                                                       |
| Recommendation     | MODIFY         | Enhance script robustness, clarify program name, document version differences           |

---
